studies.population: Adults with obesity, or overweight with at least one weight-related condition, without diabetes.
studies.safetySummary: Adverse events occurred in 73% to 94% of retatrutide groups and 70% with placebo; gastrointestinal events were most common, predominantly mild to moderate, dose related, concentrated during escalation, and partly mitigated by a 2 mg rather than 4 mg starting dose. Heart rate increased dose dependently through week 24 and then declined.

design.description: Parallel-group dose-ranging trial including fixed-dose and dose-escalation regimens.

studies.population: Adults with obesity, or overweight with at least one weight-related condition, without diabetes.
studies.safetySummary: Nausea and fatigue were the most common adverse events; the study reported no new safety signal that changed the overall benefit-risk interpretation. Serious adverse events occurred in 2-11% of eloralintide arms vs. 6% with placebo; nausea (11-64% vs. 13%) and vomiting (0-25% vs. 0%) increased with dose and were lower in the 3-9 mg dose-escalation arm than fixed 6 mg/9 mg dosing. No deaths occurred. Anti-drug antibody data were noted as collected but not reported with per-arm figures in the main text.

studies.officialTitle: A Study of Multiple Doses of NNC0487-0111 in People With Overweight or Obesity

design.description: First-in-human multiple-ascending-dose study.

studies.population: Adults with overweight or obesity without diabetes.
studies.safetySummary: All treatment-emergent adverse events were mild or moderate; gastrointestinal symptoms and decreased appetite were most common and dose related. In Part C/D (12-week dosing), serious adverse events occurred in 0% (50 mg), 0% (2x50 mg), and 6% (2x25 mg) vs. 0% placebo; nausea in 31%, 75%, and 94% vs. 8%; vomiting in 38%, 56%, and 63% vs. 0%. Across all parts (A-D), 8 of 111 (7%) participants on active treatment were anti-drug-antibody positive; no per-arm breakdown for Part C/D was reported. No deaths occurred.

studies.officialTitle: A Study of Subcutaneous Amycretin in People With Overweight or Obesity

design.description: Multiple-ascending-dose, dose-response study.

studies.population: Adults aged 18-55 years with overweight or obesity.
studies.safetySummary: A high frequency of gastrointestinal events was reported, consistent with GLP-1 and amylin agonism; no unexpected safety signal was disclosed. Serious adverse events occurred in 0-3% of amycretin arms vs. 0% placebo across parts B-E. Nausea (50-82% vs. 25-60%) and vomiting (25-53% vs. 0-60%) increased with dose. Anti-drug antibodies (low titres) occurred in 29% (Part B, 60 mg), 21% (Part C, 20 mg), and 38% (Part D, 5 mg) of active-arm participants and did not affect safety or pharmacodynamics; not reported for Part E or placebo. No deaths occurred; one case of recurrent gallstone pancreatitis was reported.

studies.officialTitle: Investigation of Safety and Efficacy of Cagrilintide in Adults With Overweight or Obesity

design.masking: Double-blind for active treatment versus pooled placebo
design.description: Dose-ranging active- and placebo-controlled trial.

studies.population: Adults with overweight or obesity.
studies.safetySummary: Gastrointestinal disorders occurred in 41%-63% across cagrilintide doses versus 32% with placebo; nausea was the most frequent event. Serious adverse events occurred in 2-7% of cagrilintide arms vs. 4% (liraglutide) and 3% (placebo); nausea (20-47% vs. 39% liraglutide, 18% placebo) and vomiting (5-9% vs. 20% liraglutide, 3% placebo) increased with cagrilintide dose. Anti-cagrilintide antibodies occurred in 46-73% of participants by week 26, increasing with dose; per-arm figures were not given in the accessible main text (only a range). No deaths occurred.

studies.officialTitle: A Phase 1 Randomized, Double Blind, Placebo Controlled, Study to Evaluate the Safety, Tolerability, Pharmacokinetics, and Pharmacodynamics of CT-388 in Otherwise Healthy Overweight and Obese Adult Participants and in Obese Patients With Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus

design.description: First-in-human sequential, multi-arm, multi-cohort SAD/MAD/MD study of subcutaneous enicepatide versus matched placebo, including cohorts with overweight or obesity without diabetes and a later cohort with obesity and type 2 diabetes.

studies.population: Adults with overweight or obesity without diabetes in the SAD, MAD, and longer multiple-dose cohorts, plus a 12-week cohort of adults with obesity and type 2 diabetes treated with diet and exercise alone or metformin; weight change was an explicit pharmacodynamic objective.
studies.safetySummary: In the published SAD cohorts, serious adverse events occurred in one participant each in the 6 mg and 7.5 mg cohorts; nausea and vomiting varied by dose. In the published MAD cohorts, no serious adverse events occurred; nausea was reported in 16.7% to 100% and vomiting in 33.3% to 83.3% of active cohorts.
